title: Unit-Level Estimation
text: Unit-level SAE models use survey microdata directly rather than starting from domain-level direct estimates. They can be powerful, but they require much stronger data conditions than area-level Fay-Herriot models.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
1 Understanding Unit-Level SAE
In area-level SAE, the model starts from one direct estimate per domain. In unit-level SAE, the model starts from individual or household survey records:
\[
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
\]
where \(y_{di}\) is the outcome for unit \(i\) in domain \(d\), \(x_{di}\) is a vector of unit-level covariates, \(u_d\) is the area effect, and \(e_{di}\) is unit-level noise. For binary outcomes, the model is usually written as a logistic or probit mixed model.
The domain estimate is then built by predicting or simulating values for the population units in each domain and aggregating them:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} y_{di}.
\]
This makes unit-level SAE a bottom-up approach: model people or households first, then aggregate to the geographic domain of choice.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
2 Pros and Cons
Unit-level SAE is useful when the survey and population data share comparable individual, household, or poststratification covariates.







Pros
Cons




Can aggregate estimates to multiple geographies
Needs detailed population data, not just area averages


Can use survey microdata directly without aggregating to domains
Survey and population variables must be coded in the same way



The main barriers to unit-level estimation are microdata availability and privacy. In general, use area-level SAE when only domain-level covariates are available (more common). Unit-level SAE becomes more realistic when suitable population microdata, or a synthetic population, can provide the prediction population.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
3 Code Implementation
Use SUMMER::smoothUnit() to fit a basic unit-level SAE model from a survey::svydesign() object and population unit-level covariates. It supports Gaussian or binomial outcomes and can optionally use an adjacency matrix for spatial structure.

library(survey)
library(SUMMER)

# survey_microdata should contain:
# - indicator
# - domain_id
# - weight and survey design variables
# - unit-level covariates used in the model
#
# population_microdata should contain:
# - domain_id
# - the same unit-level covariates for the target population

design <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~psu,
  strata = ~stratum,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_microdata,
  nest = TRUE
)

unit_fit <- SUMMER::smoothUnit(
  formula = outcome ~ age_group + employment_status + car_access,
  domain = ~domain_id,
  design = design,
  family = "binomial",
  X.pop = population_microdata,
  adj.mat = NULL,
  level = 0.95
)


3.1 Worked example
N/A

title: Data Preparation
text: The National Survey for Wales (NSW) 2022-23 respondent file provides a concrete example of the input-preparation workflow from From Estimands to SAE Inputs.

title: Data Preparation
text:
1 Setup

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

# Allows chunks to work when run from the project root or from pages/.
project_root <- if (dir.exists("pages")) "." else ".."

data_root <- file.path(project_root, "data")
raw_dir <- file.path(data_root, "raw")
census_root <- file.path(raw_dir, "census")
clean_dir <- file.path(data_root, "clean")
dir.create(clean_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

respondent_file <- file.path(
  raw_dir,
  "nsw_2022-23_respondent_file_for_ukds.tab"
)

The NSW materials used here are:

the main respondent file in data/raw;
the data user guide and variable catalogue for survey design and weight choices, as well as variable choices
Census 2021 Local Authority tables in data/raw/census

The respondent file is tab-separated. The catalogue and questionnaire use negative values such as -99, -98, -9, -8, and sometimes -7 for refused, don’t know, routed, or other non-substantive responses. Keep those visible at import, then treat them explicitly during indicator construction.

nsw_raw <- read_tsv(respondent_file, show_col_types = FALSE)

missing_codes <- c(-99, -98, -9, -8, -7)

is_missing_code <- function(x) {
  is.na(x) | x %in% missing_codes
}

make_binary <- function(x, yes_values) {
  case_when(
    is_missing_code(x) ~ NA_integer_,
    x %in% yes_values ~ 1L,
    TRUE ~ 0L
  )
}

title: Data Preparation
text:
2 Estimand
Start with a small set of estimands that illustrate different preparation decisions:

a routed travel-to-work measure,
a public transport satisfaction measure,
an active travel module measure.

Each estimand keeps the same target population and target domain: adults aged 16+ across Local Authorities.









Estimand
Indicator to be created
Target population
Target domain




Share of adults who usually use two or more modes to travel to work
commute_multimodal
Adults aged 16+
Local Authority


Share of adults dissatisfied with bus services
poor_bus_satisfaction
Adults aged 16+
Local Authority


Share of adults who walk for transport at least weekly
walk_weekly
Adults aged 16+
Local Authority

title: Data Preparation
text:
3 Indicator Construction
The indicator is the coded survey variable that measures the outcome in the estimand. The questionnaire and variable catalogue determine the source variables, routing, and response codes.








Indicator
Source variables
Construction rule




commute_multimodal
TravWkModeAll1 to TravWkModeAll8
1 when two or more commute modes are selected


poor_bus_satisfaction
BusOverSat
1 when BusOverSat is 4 or 5, meaning fairly or very dissatisfied


walk_weekly
AtFrqWlk10
1 when AtFrqWlk10 is 1, 2, or 3, meaning at least weekly



Missing and routed values are set to NA, not 0.

mode_cols <- paste0("TravWkModeAll", 1:8)

nsw_with_modes <- nsw_raw |>
  mutate(
    commute_mode_count = rowSums(
      across(all_of(mode_cols), \(x) x == 1),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    commute_mode_all_missing = if_all(all_of(mode_cols), is_missing_code)
  )

indicators <- nsw_with_modes |>
  transmute(
    unit_id = CaseNo,
    commute_multimodal = case_when(
      commute_mode_all_missing ~ NA_integer_,
      commute_mode_count >= 2 ~ 1L,
      TRUE ~ 0L
    ),
    poor_bus_satisfaction = make_binary(BusOverSat, yes_values = c(4, 5)),
    walk_weekly = make_binary(AtFrqWlk10, yes_values = c(1, 2, 3))
  )

indicators |> 
  slice_head(n = 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 4
     unit_id commute_multimodal poor_bus_satisfaction walk_weekly
       <dbl>              <int>                 <int>       <int>
 1 222300001                 NA                    NA          NA
 2 222300002                 NA                    NA          NA
 3 222300003                 NA                    NA          NA
 4 222300004                 NA                    NA          NA
 5 222300005                 NA                     1          NA
 6 222300006                 NA                     0           1
 7 222300007                 NA                     0           0
 8 222300008                 NA                     0           1
 9 222300009                  0                     0          NA
10 222300010                 NA                     1          NA


Check the amount of usable survey information for each indicator.

indicator_missingness <- indicators |>
  summarise(across(-unit_id, list(
    non_missing = \(x) sum(!is.na(x)),
    missing = \(x) sum(is.na(x)),
    positive = \(x) sum(x == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
  ))) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = c("indicator", ".value"),
    names_pattern = "(.+)_(non_missing|missing|positive)$"
  )

indicator_missingness

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  indicator                 missing positive
  <chr>                       <int>    <int>
1 commute_multimodal_non       3845       NA
2 commute_multimodal           7295      208
3 poor_bus_satisfaction_non    8918       NA
4 poor_bus_satisfaction        2222     2101
5 walk_weekly_non              1999       NA
6 walk_weekly                  9141      955

title: Data Preparation
text:
4 Target Domain
The target domain is the geography for which estimates will be produced. The NSW user guide describes results at Local Authority level, and the public respondent file contains DvLA but not a finer public geography.
Use Local Authority as the target domain.

survey_domains <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id = CaseNo,
    domain_id = str_c("W0", as.integer(DvLA) + 6000000L),
    domain_id = recode(
      domain_id,
      "W06000007" = "W06000023", # Powys
      "W06000017" = "W06000024"  # Merthyr Tydfil
    )
  )

survey_domains |>
  count(domain_id, name = "sample_n") |>
  arrange(domain_id)

# A tibble: 22 × 2
   domain_id sample_n
   <chr>        <int>
 1 W06000001      368
 2 W06000002      465
 3 W06000003      379
 4 W06000004      319
 5 W06000005      467
 6 W06000006      397
 7 W06000008      325
 8 W06000009      409
 9 W06000010      573
10 W06000011      749
# ℹ 12 more rows








Note



The domain choice is driven by the data. Ward, MSOA, or LSOA estimates would require finer respondent geography, or a documented linkage from respondents to those geographies, plus matching domain sizes and covariates.

title: Data Preparation
text:
5 Domain Size
The domain size must match the target population in the estimand. All three selected estimands use adults aged 16+ as the target population, so the required domain_size is the adult population by Local Authority.
The Census 2021 TS007 age table provides the adult population denominator.

domain_sizes <- read_csv(
  file.path(census_root, "census2021-ts007-ltla.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size =
      `Age: Total; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 4 years and under; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 5 to 9 years; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 10 to 15 years; measures: Value`
  )

domain_sizes |>
  arrange(domain_id)

# A tibble: 22 × 3
   domain_id domain_name      domain_size
   <chr>     <chr>                  <dbl>
 1 W06000001 Isle of Anglesey       57146
 2 W06000002 Gwynedd                97974
 3 W06000003 Conwy                  96364
 4 W06000004 Denbighshire           78907
 5 W06000005 Flintshire            127581
 6 W06000006 Wrexham               110270
 7 W06000008 Ceredigion             61387
 8 W06000009 Pembrokeshire         102555
 9 W06000010 Carmarthenshire       155484
10 W06000011 Swansea               197864
# ℹ 12 more rows

write_csv(domain_sizes, file.path(clean_dir, "pop_estimate.csv"))

title: Data Preparation
text:
6 Survey Weight
The weight must match the target population and the relevant survey module:

commute_multimodal uses WalesAdultWeight;
poor_bus_satisfaction uses WalesAdultWeight;
walk_weekly uses the active travel module weight, SampleTravelWeight, scaled to the adult population total.


wales_adults <- sum(domain_sizes$domain_size, na.rm = TRUE)

weights <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id = CaseNo,
    WalesAdultWeight,
    WalesTravelWeight =
      SampleTravelWeight * wales_adults / sum(SampleTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

weight_summary <- weights |>
  summarise(
    WalesAdultWeight_total = sum(WalesAdultWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    WalesTravelWeight_total = sum(WalesTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    adult_domain_size_total = wales_adults
  )

weight_summary

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  WalesAdultWeight_total WalesTravelWeight_total adult_domain_size_total
                   <dbl>                   <dbl>                   <dbl>
1               2549203.                 2559433                 2559433








Warning



Scaling weights is an analysis choice. It is used here because direct estimation uses known domain sizes. If official grossing weights are supplied for the exact target population, use those instead. If the weight scale is uncertain, consider a ratio estimator.

title: Data Preparation
text:
7 SAE Input Files and Outputs
The final preparation step writes files for direct estimation. Each estimand needs a survey indicator file with:

unit_id;
domain_id;
indicator;
weight.

The raw NSW names, such as CaseNo, are useful while preparing the data. The output files use the common SAE field names from the toolkit.
The file register records the choices used to build each output.



Indicator
Weight
Domain size




commute_multimodal
WalesAdultWeight
domain_size


poor_bus_satisfaction
WalesAdultWeight
domain_size


walk_weekly
WalesTravelWeight
domain_size




analysis_data <- indicators |>
  left_join(survey_domains, by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(weights, by = "unit_id")

for (i in seq_len(nrow(file_register))) {
  spec <- file_register[i, ]

  indicator_file <- analysis_data |>
    transmute(
      unit_id,
      domain_id,
      indicator = .data[[spec$indicator]],
      weight = .data[[spec$weight]]
    )

  write_csv(
    indicator_file,
    file.path(clean_dir, str_c("ind_weight_", spec$indicator, ".csv"))
  )
}

Check one indicator file and the domain-size file.

read_csv(
  file.path(clean_dir, "ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  glimpse()

Rows: 11,146
Columns: 4
$ unit_id   <dbl> 222300001, 222300002, 222300003, 222300004, 222300005, 22230…
$ domain_id <chr> "W06000001", "W06000001", "W06000001", "W06000001", "W060000…
$ indicator <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 1, 0, 1, NA, NA, 0, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, …
$ weight    <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 690.6047, 508.3390, 1090.8056, NA, NA, 7…

read_csv(
  file.path(clean_dir, "pop_estimate.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  glimpse()

Rows: 22
Columns: 3
$ domain_id   <chr> "W06000001", "W06000002", "W06000003", "W06000004", "W0600…
$ domain_name <chr> "Isle of Anglesey", "Gwynedd", "Conwy", "Denbighshire", "F…
$ domain_size <dbl> 57146, 97974, 96364, 78907, 127581, 110270, 61387, 102555,…


The preparation produces:

one domain-size file, data/clean/pop_estimate.csv;
three survey indicator files in data/clean/;
a documented link from each output back to the estimand, indicator construction, target domain, domain size, and weight decision.

That documentation is part of the SAE input. Without it, the estimate is difficult to interpret and easy to misuse.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text: SAE Toolkit Lite is for quick adaptation of the materials to another survey, geography, or indicator. It assumes an area-level SAE workflow and a Bayesian modelling approach.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
1 Minimum inputs and recommended schema
At minimum, an SAE workflow for Urban Analytics needs:

a survey indicator file, with one row per sampled unit used in the estimate
a domain-size file, with one row per target domain
a covariate file, when fitting an area-level model
boundary geometry to map results or drive spatial structure for spatially-informed models


1.1 Survey indicator file
A survey indicator file links each sampled unit to the chosen outcome, target domain, and survey weight. Survey documentation should be kept alongside the file so that routing, missingness, design variables, and weight choice can be checked.



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code.


indicator
Numeric outcome, or binary 0/1.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.





1.2 Domain-size file
A domain-size file gives the denominator for each target domain. The denominator must match the estimand’s target population rather than the full resident population by default.



Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


domain_size
Denominator matching the target population.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.





1.3 Geometry file
Boundary geometry is useful for visualising estimates and for deriving adjacency structures for spatial models. It is not required for direct estimates or non-spatial area-level models.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code, matching the domain-size file.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.


geometry
Polygon or multipolygon geometry for the reporting domain.





1.4 Covariate file for area-level estimation
Covariates are area-level predictors available for every target domain. They should be measured on the same geography as the direct estimates, or aggregated to that geography before modelling.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


covariate_*
Area-level predictor available for every target domain.


median_age
Example demographic covariate.


prop_urban
Example settlement or land-use covariate.


employment_rate
Example socioeconomic covariate.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
2 Downloadable scripts
Reusable scripts can be adapted for another survey once the input files follow the schema above.



Script
Purpose




direct_estimates.R
Calculate direct estimates by domain, sampling variances, and confidence intervals.


covariate_sae.R
Fit Bayesian area-level models using direct estimates, sampling variances, and area-level covariates.


spatial_sae.R
Build adjacency or spatial weight matrices from domain boundaries and fit Bayesian spatial SAE models.


diagnostics.R
Create comparison tables and plots for checking estimates, uncertainty, and model outputs.

title: Frequentist Area-Level Estimation
text: Frequentist area-level SAE is the next step when direct estimates are too noisy or unstable, or when there are domains with weak survey information. The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model, which combines direct estimates with domain-level covariates to improve precision.

title: Frequentist Area-Level Estimation
text:
1 Understanding the Fay-Herriot model
Fay-Herriot combines two pieces of information for each domain:

the direct estimate from the survey, and
a covariate-based model prediction.

The direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) can be written as the covariate-explained part \(x_d^\top\beta\), plus an area-specific unexplained effect \(u_d\), plus survey sampling noise \(e_d\).
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]
The FH estimate, is a weighted average of the direct estimate and the covariate-based prediction:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d =
\hat{\gamma}_d \hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+
(1-\hat{\gamma}_d) x_d^\top\hat{\beta}.
\]
The shrinkage weight \(\hat{\gamma}_d = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_u^2}{\hat{\sigma}_u^2 + \psi_d}\), where \(\psi_d\) is the sampling variance of the direct estimate, controls how much the FH estimate “trusts” the direct estimate:

if \(\psi_d\) is small, the direct estimate is precise, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 1;
if \(\psi_d\) is large, the direct estimate is noisy, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 0.

In practice, FH leaves precise direct estimates mostly unchanged, while pulling noisy direct estimates toward the covariate-based prediction.






Note



In this frequentist FH workflow, the main outputs are the model-based estimate (EBLUP) and its uncertainty (RMSE, the square root of model-based MSE). For domains without a usable direct estimate, the EBLUP comes entirely from the model component.

title: Frequentist Area-Level Estimation
text:
2 Covariate Choices
Domain-level covariates can often be found via census datas and other population-level aggregate statistics.Choose covariates with emphasis on coverage and comparability across domains.

Coverage: each covariate should be available for all target domains.
Alignment: match geography, definition, and reference period to the survey indicator.
Relevance: keep variables with a clear substantive link to the outcome and avoid including many weak predictors.
Predictive: choose covariates that improve domain-level prediction and are easy to explain; avoid downstream variables, and remove or combine highly correlated predictors.

Common examples of covariates when estimating transport-related indicators are: age, income, employment profile, population density, housing mix, deprivation, urban/rural classification, transport accessbility metrics, car ownership, etc.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Frequentist Area-Level Estimation
text:
3 Evaluation
Evaluate the FH outputs (EBLUPs) and uncertainty (RMSEs) to decide how to proceed:



Scenario
Next step




Estimates are stable, and uncertainty acceptable
Use EBLUPs for reporting.


Results are sensitive to covariate choice, coefficients are unstable, or diagnostics show poor fit
Revisit covariates and refit FH.


A probability-style uncertainty would help interpretation
Move to Bayesian Area-Level SAE (+Spatial) to represent uncertainty through posterior estimates and Credible Intervals.


Residuals show spatial autocorrelation after the covariate FH model
Consider adding spatial structure to borrow strength from neighbouring areas. See Bayesian Area-Level SAE (+Spatial).

title: Frequentist Area-Level Estimation
text:
4 Implementation in R

4.1 Functions
Use sae::eblupFH() to fit a frequentist Fay-Herriot model and sae::mseFH() to estimate uncertainty.

library(sae)

# direct_results should contain: 
# - domain_id, 
# - Direct, 
# - Variance 
#
# domain_covariates should contain: 
# - domain_id, 
# - area-level covariates

fh_data <- merge(
  direct_results,
  domain_covariates,
  by = "domain_id"
)

# Fit Fay-Herriot EBLUPs + RMSEs
fh_fit <- sae::eblupFH(
  formula = Direct ~ x_income + x_car_ownership + x_urban,
  vardir = fh_data$Variance,
  data = fh_data
)

fh_mse <- sae::mseFH(
  formula = Direct ~ x_income + x_car_ownership + x_urban,
  vardir = fh_data$Variance,
  data = fh_data
)

# Combine EBLUP + RMSE outputs for reporting
fh_results <- data.frame(
  domain_id = fh_data$domain_id,
  direct = fh_data$Direct,
  eblup = fh_fit$eblup,
  mse = fh_mse$mse,
  rmse = sqrt(fh_mse$mse)
)



4.2 Worked example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text: Diagnostics decide whether SAE outputs are stable, interpretable, and suitable for reporting. They should compare the survey-only signal, the modelled estimates, and the uncertainty attached to each result.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
1 Model Comparison
Compare direct estimates, non-spatial Fay-Herriot estimates, Bayesian estimates, and spatial estimates where available. The aim is not to choose the smoothest map, but to understand what the model has changed.
Useful checks include:

direct estimate versus model estimate,
domains with the largest shrinkage,
changes between non-spatial and spatial models,
residual pattern and remaining spatial autocorrelation,
sensitivity to covariates and modelling choices,
whether model estimates aggregate sensibly to known higher-level totals.

When teaching with simulated or complete-population data, ground-truth comparison can be useful. For real survey applications, use direct estimates, external benchmarks, known totals, held-out checks where feasible, and substantive plausibility.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
2 Uncertainty
Direct estimates, frequentist FH models, and Bayesian models use different uncertainty summaries. Avoid treating them as interchangeable.







Workflow
Common uncertainty output




Direct estimation
sampling variance, standard error, confidence interval, relative standard error


Frequentist FH
MSE, RMSE, approximate confidence interval


Bayesian FH / spatial model
posterior interval, credible interval, exceedance probability



Map uncertainty as well as point estimates. Flag domains with high uncertainty, very small sample sizes, unstable weights, or estimates driven mostly by the model rather than local survey information.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
3 Benchmarking and Reporting
Benchmarking adjusts estimates so they agree with known totals at a higher geography, such as national or regional totals. It is useful when consistency with official aggregates matters, but it should be documented because it changes the model output.
Reporting should make clear:

the estimand and target population,
the domain geography,
whether estimates are direct, model-based, or benchmarked,
which covariates and model family were used,
how uncertainty should be read,
which domains should be interpreted cautiously.

Do not present smoothed or modelled estimates as if they were observed survey results.






Tip



Useful R functions depend on the fitted model. In sae, mseFH() estimates MSE for Fay-Herriot EBLUPs and pbmseBHF() can be used for bootstrap MSE in Battese-Harter-Fuller workflows. Base R and tidyverse tools are usually enough for comparison tables once estimates are in a common format.
Documentation: sae::mseFH(), sae::pbmseBHF()

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
4 Worked example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example here, no need for live code block
National Survey of Wales

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
Beginner-friendly Small Area Estimation (SAE) for researchers estimating survey-derived metrics across a whole country or region at fine geographic resolution.
The site has two tracks: a theory track that explains the SAE workflow, and a practical tutorial track that applies the workflow to a dataset. It uses the National Survey for Wales as the first worked survey.
The main practical uses R, Quarto, and SAE packages such as survey, sae, SUMMER, and INLA.Make sure you have R version 4.5.1 installed. You can download it from CRAN.

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
1 Why Small Area Estimation?
[draft] Survey are often not precise enough for fine geographies such as Local Authorities, MSOAs, or LSOAs. Some areas have very few/no respondents, unequal weights Taking weighted meands can be unstable. Also out of domain sample
SAE is useful when direct estimates are too noisy or incomplete. Area-level SAE use direct estimates and their sampling variances, borrow strength across areas using covariates and random effects.
SAE can be further improved with bayesian statistics (to smooth out area) and add spatial structure (spatial autocorrelation)
SAE combines survey data, population estimates, auxiliary data, modelling to estimate the unsurveyed in an informed way.
[add an image that represents SAE]

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
2 Methods
Use these pages to understand the concepts and decisions behind the workflow.



No.
Step
Objective




1
Preparing the Inputs
Define what is being estimated, for whom, and at what geographical scale.


2
Direct estimation
Estimate area-level indicators directly from the survey and assess their precision.


3
Frequentist Area-Level SAE
Fit a Fay-Herriot model that improves unstable direct estimates by borrowing strength from covariates.


4
Bayesian Area-Level SAE (+Spatial)
Reframe the area-level model with posterior uncertainty, then add spatial structure to borrow strength from neighbouring areas.


5
Diagnose and Report
Check model performance, report uncertainty, and present estimates.

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
3 Resources
Supporting materials for adapting the workflow:

SAE Toolkit collects reusable input formats and helper scripts.
Troubleshooting lists common data, estimation, and reporting problems.

title: Bayesian Area-Level Estimation (+Spatial)
text: This notebook extends the area-level Fay-Herriot workflow. It first reframes area-level SAE in Bayesian terms, then shows why spatial structure may be useful when neighbouring domains share unexplained patterns.

title: Bayesian Area-Level Estimation (+Spatial)
section: 1 Bayesian versus Frequentist approach in SAE
text:
1 Bayesian versus Frequentist approach in SAE
Both frequentist and Bayesian area-level SAE start from the same basic Fay-Herriot idea:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]
The difference is how the unknown quantities are estimated and how uncertainty is interpreted.

In the frequentist approach, \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and area effects are estimated as fixed unknowns. Typical outputs are one point estimate per area (EBLUP), plus uncertainty measures such as RMSE, and confidence intervals.
In the Bayesian approach, unknown quantities such as \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(u_d\) are treated as uncertain and assigned prior distributions. Typical outputs are posterior means or medians, credible intervals, and probability summaries.

This means that, with a Bayesian approach, each area has a distribution of plausible values. This makes it natural to report a central estimate, an uncertainty interval, and probability-style summaries, such as how likely an area is to be above a certain policy threshold.






Note




Use the frequentist Fay-Herriot workflow as the simpler starting point when you need stable area estimates, RMSEs, and a transparent model-based improvement over direct estimates.
Use a Bayesian workflow when probability-style summaries are useful for the decision, or when you want to add spatial structure.



[ADD IMAGE]

title: Bayesian Area-Level Estimation (+Spatial)
text:
2 Spatial Structure
Let’s go back to the FH model equation cited above. The non-spatial area-level model assumes the unexplained area effects \(u_d\) are independent after accounting for covariates. In urban research, this is rarely true: Neighbouring places may share more similarities that could help inform our estimates.
Spatial area-level models add a structured area effect using an adjacency matrix or neighbourhood graph. This lets the model borrow strength not only from covariates, but also from nearby areas.
The spatial structure is usually provided as a spatial weights matrix: a table that tells the model which areas are connected, and how strongly. The simplest spatial weights matrix is a binary adjacency matrix. Each row and column represents a domain. A value of 1 means two domains are neighbours, and 0 means they are not. Neighbours are usually defined by shared boundaries, for example Queen contiguity or Rook contiguity, or distance threshold.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Bayesian Area-Level Estimation (+Spatial)
text:
3 Evaluation
At this stage, it is worth comparing the evolving outputs of the estimates and their uncertainties from direct estimates through each addition of covariates and spatial information.The aim is simply to check whether the extra modelling is useful and defensible.
Focus on three checks:

Estimates: do posterior estimates broadly agree with reliable direct estimates and frequentist EBLUPs?
Uncertainty: do credible intervals or threshold probabilities make the results easier to interpret than RMSE alone?
Spatial effect: if spatial smoothing changes the map, is that supported by residual spatial autocorrelation and substantive knowledge?

If the Bayesian or spatial model changes results substantially, check priors, covariates, direct variances, influential domains, and the adjacency structure before reporting.

title: Bayesian Area-Level Estimation (+Spatial)
text:
4 Code Implementation
For Bayesian area-level SAE, the main package used here is SUMMER. It fits area-level smoothing models using INLA, and can work either from direct estimates or from survey microdata.

Use SUMMER::smoothArea() when you already have direct estimates and their variances.
Use SUMMER::smoothSurvey() when you want SUMMER to calculate design-adjusted direct estimates from survey microdata before smoothing. Both can use an adjacency matrix for spatial structure.


library(SUMMER)

# direct_results should contain: 
# - domain_id, 
# - Direct, 
# - Variance 
#
# domain_covariates should contain: 
# - domain_id, 
# - area-level covariates
#
# adjacency_matrix should have row and column names matching domain_id

direct_est <- direct_results[, c("domain_id", "Direct", "Variance")]

bayes_area_fit <- SUMMER::smoothArea(
  formula = Direct ~ x_income + x_car_ownership + x_urban,
  domain = ~domain_id,
  direct.est = direct_est,
  X.domain = domain_covariates,
  adj.mat = adjacency_matrix,       # if NULL, the model is non-spatial
  transform = "identity",           # no data transformation of estimates
  level = 0.95                      # CI level
)


4.1 Worked example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example here National Survey of Wales

title: Direct Estimation
text: Direct estimates are the first estimation step in an area-level SAE workflow. They show what the survey can say before borrowing strength from covariates or spatial structure.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Inputs
The direct-estimation step uses the standard files prepared earlier:







File
Required fields




Survey indicator file
unit_id, domain_id, indicator, weight


Domain-size file
domain_id, domain_size



The indicator column can be binary, such as 1 for adults who walk weekly and 0 otherwise, or numeric for a continuous outcome.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Direct Estimator and Sampling Variances
The estimator should match the information available in the survey file and the estimand definition.








Situation
Estimator
When to use




Domain size is known and grossing weight is available
Horvitz-Thompson estimator
Anchors estimates to true domain size and gives design-unbiased levels; required for estimating counts or totals


Domain size is unknown and/or only analysis weights are available
Hájek estimator
Preferred for means or proportions when using normalised analysis weights and no reliable domain size is available



The Horvitz-Thompson estimator calculates the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) in domain (\(d\)) as:
\[
\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR} =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di} y_{di}}{N_d}
\]
The Hájek Estimator calculates the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d\) in domain (\(d\)) as:
\[
\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR} =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di} y_{di}}
{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}}.
\]
where \(N_d\) is domain_size, \(w_{di}\) is weight, and \(y_{di}\) is indicator.
Direct estimates are paired with estimated sampling variances, telling us how much uncertainty comes from observing only a sample within each domain. In practice, the variance depends on the estimator, weight scale, and survey design information available.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Evaluation
Evaluate the direct estimates and variances outputs to make a decision how to proceed:



Scenario
Next step




Most domains have usable sample sizes and uncertainty is acceptable
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Many domains have no sample, or invalid variances
Revisit the domain choice or redefine estimand.


Variances are acceptable but poor precision
Proceed to Frequentist Area-Level SAE to ‘tighten’ the estimates and their variances



(*)Here, “usable variance” means a finite, positive sampling variance for the direct estimate in each domain.
In case Model-based SAE is necessary, the output needed will be:

domain_id
Direct, the survey-only estimate
Variance, the estimated sampling variance of Direct

title: Direct Estimation
text:
4 Implementation in R

4.1 Functions
Use sae::direct() to calculate direct estimates by domain. It returns core direct-estimation outputs including domain sample size (SampSize), direct estimate (Direct), estimated standard deviation (SD), and coefficient of variation (CV). Square SD to obtain the Variance.

library(sae)

direct_fit <- sae::direct(
  y = survey_indicator$indicator,
  dom = survey_indicator$domain_id,
  sweight = survey_indicator$weight,
  domsize = domain_sizes, 
  replace = FALSE, # without replacement (i.e., no duplicate respondents)
)

direct_results <- direct_fit |>
  dplyr::rename(domain_id = Domain) |>
  dplyr::mutate(Variance = SD^2)







Note



In sae::direct(), if domsize is provided, the Horvitz-Thompson direct estimator is used; if not provided, the Hajek ratio direct estimator is used.


If the survey includes more detailed design information, use the survey package for the direct-estimation step. This gives design-aware domain estimates and uncertainty summaries.

library(survey)

design <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~psu,                 # primary sampling unit
  strata = ~stratum,          # survey stratum ID
  weights = ~weight,          # survey weight
  data = survey_indicator,    # survey indicator file
)

direct_design_fit <- survey::svyby(
  formula = ~indicator,       
  by = ~domain_id,            
  design = design,            
  FUN = svymean,  # other values: svytotal, svyratio, etc.
  vartype = c("se", "ci"),    # output SE, CI, CV
  na.rm = TRUE
)



4.2 Worked example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text: A survey outcome becomes SAE-ready through a clear estimand, a coded indicator, a target domain, a domain size, and a compatible survey weight. To see this workflow in action, go to Tutorial: Data Preparation.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
1 Defining the Estimand
Before fitting any SAE model, define exactly what the survey estimate should represent. This so-called estimand determines how SAE-ready inputs are prepared and how to choose the most appropriate estimation approach downstream. It is good practice to formulate the estimands of interest in this format:

Estimate the measure of what outcome, among which population, across which geographic domains.

E.g.,

Share of adults who use the bus weekly across MSOA
Mean commute distance among workers across Wards

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
2 Constructing the Indicator
After defining the estimand, identify or create the survey indicator that measures the outcome. Here are some of the most common indicator types:








Indicator type
What to define
Example




Binary
What is 1, 0, missing, and out of scope. Also suitable for categorical variables
Estimand: Share of adults who use the bus weeklyIndicator: Uses bus weekly: yes/no


Continuous
The numeric value and unit
Estimand: Mean commute distance among workers Indicator: Commute distance in miles



Use the survey questionnaire, response codes, and routing rules to guide indicator construction. Some indicators are direct recodes of one survey question; others need to combine several variables. The same raw survey variable can support different estimands.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
section: 3 Choosing Domain Sizes and Survey Weights
text:
3 Choosing Domain Sizes and Survey Weights

3.1 Domain size
The domain size \(N_d\) is the size of the target population in each domain \(d\). It is the denominator for domain means and proportions, and is essential for the SAE pipeline. Domain sizes can often be obtained from census datasets or other regional aggregate statistics.

The target population is the group the estimate is about, such as all adults, households, workers, commuters, or public transport users, etc.
The target domain is the geographic area for which estimates will be produced, such as Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA, etc.

The choice of target domain, besides being shaped by the estimand definition, should also be informed by data availability. For example,

if survey respondent records are only identified at the Local Authority level and no finer, then Local Authority is the most granular target domain for SAE
if the domain sizes are only available at the Ward level and no finer (e.g., total worker population by Ward), then it is recommended to use Ward as the most granular target domain for SAE

[ADD IMAGE]


3.2 Survey weight
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent real-world proportions. The Survey User Guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, how they are derived, and which survey modules/questions they apply to. In general, weight variables are derived to adjust for one or more aspects, such as:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For a SAE workflow, there is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection. The weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. Moreover, it is also important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable, as it implies different estimation approaches.

Some weights are grossing weights \(w^{G}\). They make proportions representative of the target population and sum to a population total or domain size \(N_d\).
Other weights are analysis weights \(w^{A}\). They make proportions representative of the target population, but often sum to the achieved sample size \(n_d\).

If analysis weights are provided and the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula \(w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}\) if required.
[ADD IMAGE]






Note



For the SAE workflow, the preferred setup is one where grossing weights are provided for each survey respondent record and domain sizes are available, because it gives a direct link between the survey respondent records and the population count in each domain.
If the domain size is unknown and/or no compatible weight variables are provided for the estimand of interest, alternative direct estimation methods may be used. Otherwise, the estimand may also be redefined (e.g., coarser geography, less specific target population).

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
4 Preparing SAE inputs
Putting everything together, each of the estimands of interest should have a short record as follows that will guide how we prepare input datasets for the SAE workflow. For example, a compact record could be written as:













#
Estimand
Indicator
Indicator construction
Target domain
Domain size
Weight variable
Other remarks




1
Share of adults who use the bus weekly across MSOAs
is_freq_bus_user
Binary: 1 if BusUseFreq = 1; 0 if adult does not use the bus weekly; null for invalid or refused responses
MSOA
Adult population aged 16+ by MSOA
AdultWeight; applies to all adult respondents; grossing weight
Stratified by age_group


2
Mean commute distance among workers across Wards
commute_distance
Continuous: numeric miles from TravelWorkDistance; only workers with valid commute distance are in scope
Ward
Worker population by Ward
CommuteModuleWeight; applies to commute module respondents (a subsample)
—



Finally, each estimand should have enough information to create:

a survey indicator file with one row per sampled unit and the fields unit_id, domain_id, indicator, and weight;
a domain-size file with one row per target domain and the fields domain_id, domain_size (optionally, domain_name);

title: Troubleshooting
text: Lorem Ipsum

title: Estimation and Diagnostics
text:
The estimation workflow starts from the prepared SAE inputs from the National Survey for Wales example. It will cover:

calculating direct estimates and sampling variances,
checking sample sizes and unstable domains,
fitting area-level SAE models,
comparing direct and modelled estimates,
diagnosing uncertainty and residual patterns,
preparing outputs for reporting.
